summary: Gruber examines the Superhuman email client controversy, where the app embedded tracking pixels in outgoing emails by default, showing senders when and where recipients read messages. While Superhuman made some changes after Mike Davidson's exposé, Gruber argues the fixes were superficial and the core privacy violation remains. He draws a key distinction between legitimate read receipts (controlled by recipients) and Superhuman's surveillance pixels (controlled by senders). Gruber traces the root problem to email clients acting as web browsers that load remote content, arguing this opened email to every web privacy exploit. He calls on email clients to defend users by loading remote images through proxy servers, rather than relying on senders to behave ethically.
key_points: Superhuman's tracking pixels function as sender-controlled surveillance, fundamentally different from legitimate read receipts which are under the recipient's control
The vast majority of people don't realize email tracking is possible and incorrectly assume email is completely private like paper mail
The root cause is that email clients became de facto web browsers, importing every web privacy exploit into a medium people assume is private
No closed messaging platform allows sender-controlled read receipts, making Superhuman's approach an outlier even by industry standards
The ultimate fix must come from email clients themselves, which should load remote images anonymously through proxies rather than relying on sender goodwill
money_quotes: Email should be private, and most people assume, incorrectly, that it is.
Once we allowed email clients to act as de facto web browsers, loading remote content from servers when messages are viewed, we opened up not just a can of worms but an entire case of canned worms.
Every privacy exploit for a web browser is now a privacy exploit for email. But it's worse, because people naturally assume that email is completely private.
Read receipts should be under the control of the recipient, not the sender. Full stop.
Email should be every bit as private as people assume that it is.
No closed messaging platform that I'm aware of allows for read receipts that are controlled by the sender, not the recipient.
This is not 'Mission Accomplished' for ethical product design or privacy — at all.
key_insight: Email's transformation from plain text to a de facto web browser created a fundamental privacy crisis, and the fix must come from email clients defending recipients rather than trusting senders to behave ethically.
